questions.question: Explain Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) in paging systems. How does it improve address translation speed? Differentiate between TLB hit and TLB miss with suitable example.
keyPoints: TLB is a hardware cache that stores recent page table entries
Reduces memory access time by avoiding page table lookup
TLB hit: fast access, TLB miss: requires page table access + update TLB
Effective Access Time = Hit ratio × (Tlb + Memory) + Miss ratio × (Tlb + 2×Memory)

questions.question: Compare segmentation and paging memory management techniques. List two advantages and two disadvantages of each approach.
keyPoints: Segmentation provides logical division; paging provides physical division
Segmentation eliminates internal fragmentation; paging eliminates external fragmentation
Segmentation supports protection per segment; paging uses same protection for all pages
Combined approach uses segmentation for logical view and paging for physical memory management

questions.question: Consider a system with combined segmentation and paging. The logical address is generated using 16-bit segment number and 16-bit offset. The segment table entry contains: valid bit, protection bits, and page table base register. Each segment is divided into pages of size 4KB. The physical memory has 1024 frames. (i) Calculate the logical address format showing segment index, page number, and page offset. (ii) For logical address 0x2A3B4C5D, show step-by-step address translation to find physical address. (iii) Determine the number of bits required for physical address.
keyPoints: Logical address format: Seg#(16 bits) | Page#(offset bits) | Offset(12 bits for 4KB page)
Offset = 12 bits (2^12 = 4096), Page size = 4KB, so remaining bits for page number
Translation: Segment number → Segment Table → Page Table Base → Page Table → Frame Number → Physical Address

questions.question: A computer system has 4 frames and uses LRU page replacement algorithm. The page reference string is: 7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1, 7, 0, 1. (i) Show the state of memory after each page reference and calculate total page faults. (ii) Now assume a TLB with 4 entries having hit ratio of 70%. Memory access time is 100ns and TLB lookup takes 20ns. Calculate the effective memory access time. (iii) Explain how inverted page tables differ from traditional page tables and when they are preferred.
keyPoints: LRU replaces least recently used page; count page faults for each new page not in memory
Inverted page table: one entry per physical frame instead of virtual page; reduces memory overhead
Inverted page tables used in large address spaces; requires hash table for virtual-to-physical mapping
